starting_state.openness_summary: Larry picks up without hostility but immediately signals a qualification gap — he doesn't know Ion and is operating on a different expectation (Tesla product).
starting_state.quote: I'm doing alright. I'm not familiar with Ion.
starting_state.reasoning: The line is neither resistant nor warm. Larry is willing to engage but his frame is already misaligned — he expected Tesla, not a solar company he doesn't recognize. That misalignment is the structural problem Jake never fully resolves.

evidence.quote: Yeah. So we're just a solar company. We work in your area. Install quite a bit, over there. We'd love to get you out a free, solar design so you can kinda see what we could do for you.
evidence.reasoning: Jake opens without the Tesla-certified-installer framing the codex requires for Tesla-sourced leads. He responds to Larry's 'not familiar' comment by shrinking the pitch rather than anchoring legitimacy. This is reactive from word one.

evidence.quote: To be honest with you, I'm just in in really in scheduling. So my job is to kinda just verify all this and then connect you with my specialist, and they can give you a call.
evidence.reasoning: Jake correctly states his role here, which is a moment of genuine strategic awareness — but it arrives only after Larry has already pulled him into a battery-price question. The scope boundary was reactive, not proactive.

evidence.quote: Yeah. I mean, you got our number, so you can give us a callback if, it is something that you're kinda trying to look into when, you do get that roof. Alright?
evidence.reasoning: The single moment where a cross-sell bridge to Ion's in-house roofing was available, Jake disengaged. No strategic setup follows — he simply surrenders the lead.

state_changes.quote: So the only thing I remember signing up for or or filling out was for Tesla. Are you are you, like, a subcontractor of Tesla?
state_changes.reasoning: Larry's first active probe. He begins testing whether Jake is who he says he is. The expectation gap (Tesla vs. Ion) surfaces here and Jake never closes it cleanly.

state_changes.quote: Well, I'm retired. So, like, I was I was under the understanding that Tesla was offering, like, free free solar roofs with, battery.
state_changes.reasoning: Larry reveals the specific promise he believed he was responding to — free Tesla solar roofs. His retirement mention adds financial context. Jake's subsequent denial of this offer without reframing shifts Larry into disappointment rather than curiosity.

state_changes.quote: But, we do have a I guess there's just false advertisement on Facebook, and that's how you got my number.
state_changes.reasoning: Larry explicitly names the source of his frustration — he feels he was misled by the ad. Jake's response at 03:13 confirms the data-routing without any trust repair, which deepens the resentment.

state_changes.quote: Is that something y'all do? It's something I think I could probably do myself, actually.
state_changes.reasoning: Larry's question about ground-mounted systems is his most curious moment on the call. He is actively problem-solving his east-west roof orientation, which signals real interest in solar if the technical fit can be found. Jake's flat 'we don't do ground mount' deflates this window.

state_changes.quote: So in other words, y'all y'all bought my information from some other company. Right?
state_changes.reasoning: Larry's phrasing here is pointed but not explosive — he is arriving at a conclusion he already suspected. The data-mining grievance cements his sense that this call is not what he expected and that Ion is not a trustworthy actor.

state_changes.quote: And, honestly, it doesn't sound like you've you've done me, satellite check on on the situation of my house before you even made the call.
state_changes.reasoning: Larry's final pivot to criticizing Jake's preparation is not primarily about the satellite check. It is the customer's way of closing the door while maintaining his technical dignity. He has found a procedural reason to end the call that does not require him to say 'I don't trust you.'

multi_touch_patterns.pattern_name: Tesla-sourced lead / identity and legitimacy challenge

touches.quote: I'm doing alright. I'm not familiar with Ion.
touches.rep_handling: Jake responded by describing Ion as 'just a solar company' with no Tesla framing — undersold the relationship.

touches.quote: So the only thing I remember signing up for or or filling out was for Tesla. Are you are you, like, a subcontractor of Tesla?
touches.rep_handling: Jake confirmed the installer-partner relationship but immediately pivoted to battery pricing before anchoring legitimacy.

touches.quote: I was I was under the understanding that Tesla was offering, like, free free solar roofs with, battery.
touches.rep_handling: Jake denied the free-roof claim without acknowledging the expectation gap or reframing what Ion actually offers.

touches.quote: So I, like, filled out stuff on a thing through supposedly Tesla that was offering like, they were looking for customers to try out this new new they call them, like, shingles.
touches.rep_handling: Jake offered no response — the call effectively ended here as Larry concluded he had been misled.

multi_touch_patterns.stewart_read: Larry raised the Tesla-identity question four times in six minutes. Each time Jake either partially acknowledged, deflected to product detail, or stayed silent. The codex provides a clean reframe for this exact scenario (intros.tesla, context.tesla_relationship_clarification) — 'Tesla referred your inquiry to us as their installer partner in Virginia; we install Ion panels plus the Tesla Powerwall battery option; the closer walks through the full package.' Jake never deployed it. By the fourth touch, Larry had self-concluded that he'd been baited. This pattern is the structural collapse of the call — not the roof objection.

touches.quote: I guess there's just false advertisement on Facebook, and that's how you got my number.
touches.rep_handling: Jake moved immediately to the product pitch without addressing the data-origin concern.

touches.quote: So in other words, y'all y'all bought my information from some other company. Right?
touches.rep_handling: Jake confirmed the lead-source routing matter-of-factly: 'From, the lead sources you filled out, yeah, they did send it to a a few companies there.' No trust repair attempted.

touches.quote: Data mining is is, like, the biggest biggest money making thing there is in in the world today.
touches.rep_handling: Jake's only response was 'Yeah. It is' — a full concession that framed Ion as a data-mining operation.

multi_touch_patterns.stewart_read: Larry's data-mining concern was not a technical objection — it was a trust signal asking for reassurance that Ion is a legitimate company acting in good faith. The codex handles the 'how did you get my number' variant under protocols.how_did_you_get_my_number: honest specificity reduces suspicion; vagueness increases it. Jake's confirmation that lead sources 'did send it to a few companies' without any legitimacy anchor or reframe essentially validated Larry's worst interpretation. The 'Yeah. It is' concession on data mining is the lowest point of the call from a trust standpoint.

multi_touch_patterns.pattern_name: Technical scope creep — customer-initiated equipment debate

touches.quote: Is that something y'all do? It's something I think I could probably do myself, actually.
touches.rep_handling: Jake engaged the ground-mount question directly, then pivoted to explaining why roof leaks are no longer a concern — entering warranty and installation quality territory.

touches.quote: And are you talking about individual inverters for every single panel? Yep. I thought I thought that design was going away.
touches.rep_handling: Jake debated micro-inverter vs. string-inverter technology with a self-described electronics expert for approximately 90 seconds.

touches.quote: Alright. Well, maybe I'll contact you after I, get a roof. Cool. You don't even have an idea of what what these are running?
touches.rep_handling: Jake attempted to answer the pricing question, providing a partial explanation of the lease/third-party-ownership model without redirecting to the specialist.

multi_touch_patterns.stewart_read: Larry is a technically confident, retired, self-described 'educated science person' who debates on his own terms. Jake matched him in every technical exchange rather than holding setter scope. The codex is explicit (coaching_philosophy.setter_scope_discipline): 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer. I'm scheduling, they're solving. Let me get you on the calendar.' Each debate cost time and produced no progress toward a booked appointment. More importantly, Jake lost the debates — he did not land a knockout on ground mounts, roof leaks, or micro-inverters, and Larry knew it. Scope creep here was not just inefficient; it actively undermined Jake's credibility with an expert customer.

trajectory_verdict.summary: The call started with a recoverable legitimacy gap — Larry expected Tesla product, got Ion — and ended with that gap unresolved and compounded by scope creep, a missed roofing cross-sell, and a data-trust concession. The roof objection at 06:19 was a genuine but temporary barrier that an Ion roofing bridge would have converted into a separate revenue event; instead Jake surrendered the lead. The trajectory is consistently energy-leaking: each exchange left Larry with less trust and more reasons to disengage, until he closed the door on his own terms using the satellite-check criticism.
trajectory_verdict.outcome_dispute: The v1 tag 'no_interest / unqualified' is partially correct but imprecise. Larry is NOT disqualified — he is pre-qualified pending roof completion, and the roofing timeline is an Ion opportunity, not a dead end. A more accurate tag would be 'deferred_solar_pipeline / roofing_cross_sell_missed.' The call ended without a booking because Jake did not bridge to Ion's roofing team, not because Larry is unqualifiable.

grounded_examples.quote: I'm definitely can't, like, think about solar until I get a new roof.
grounded_examples.reasoning: This is the cross-sell trigger, not the DQ signal. The codex's cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned section covers this exact scenario — a customer who needs a new roof is a roofing lead AND a deferred solar lead, not a lost contact.

grounded_examples.quote: Yeah. I mean, you got our number, so you can give us a callback if, it is something that you're kinda trying to look into when, you do get that roof. Alright?
grounded_examples.reasoning: Jake's surrender at this moment is the single most consequential decision of the call. The roofing bridge was available; he chose the exit instead.

cherry_pick_moments.quote: Hey, man. This is Jake with Ion. I was just giving you a call here because I saw that you requested some information on solar for your house.
cherry_pick_moments.stewart_read: Jake's opening never deploys the Tesla-certified-installer framing the codex prescribes for Tesla-sourced leads. He introduces Ion as a generic solar company and references a form submission, not the Tesla referral. This is the original sin of the call — the legitimacy anchor that would have answered 'I'm not familiar with Ion' before Larry had to ask it. The Tesla framing (per intros.tesla) exists precisely to answer 'is this a scam?' in the first 30 seconds. Jake skipped it.
cherry_pick_moments.alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake may have avoided Tesla framing intentionally to prevent the product-expectation conversation early. Not supported — the product-expectation conversation happened anyway, and without the legitimacy anchor it was harder to resolve.
cherry_pick_moments.coaching_implication: Open the replay at 00:09. Ask Jake: 'What's the first thing we want Larry to know about us?' Then walk him through the Tesla-certified-installer intro as the answer to 'I'm not familiar with Ion' before the customer has to ask. This is a mechanics fix, not a philosophy fix.

cherry_pick_moments.quote: I think I have to have more trees taken down Oh, yeah. Before beforehand.
cherry_pick_moments.stewart_read: Larry volunteers — unprompted — that he has already had trees removed and has eight more scheduled. This is a customer actively managing his property to make solar viable. It is the clearest enthusiasm signal on the call. Jake acknowledges it ('Okay') and pivots to address confirmation rather than anchoring on it. The correct move here is to reflect the signal back: Larry has been planning this for a while and is removing obstacles himself — that is a buyer orientation, not a browser orientation.
cherry_pick_moments.coaching_implication: Show Jake this moment and ask him what it tells us about Larry as a customer. Use it to illustrate what 'qualifying on interest depth' looks like per verify.interest_in_solar — the Codex says top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Larry gave Jake an open door; Jake walked past it.

cherry_pick_moments.quote: We we typically get the numbers from the lead sources. We don't always control what they, put out, unfortunately.
cherry_pick_moments.stewart_read: Larry has just named 'false advertisement on Facebook' as the source of his information and implicitly accused Ion of being connected to it. Jake's response distances Ion from responsibility but does not repair the trust gap. The codex protocol for 'how did you get my number' calls for honest specificity: name the referral source cleanly and anchor Ion's legitimacy separately. Jake did neither — he issued a partial disclaimer and pivoted to the product. The result is that Larry's suspicion remains unaddressed and resurfaces at 07:21.
cherry_pick_moments.alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake may have moved quickly past the data-origin question to avoid dwelling on a topic he cannot fully resolve. Not supported — the topic recurs twice more, which is the consequence of not resolving it here.
cherry_pick_moments.coaching_implication: This is the trust repair moment Jake needed. Suggested reframe (intent, not verbatim): 'You filled out a form through Tesla's referral network — Tesla routes inquiries to us as their installer partner in Virginia. That's how we got connected. I know that's different from what the ad may have said, and I want to be straight with you about it.' Two sentences of honesty here likely prevent the data-mining exchange at 07:38.

cherry_pick_moments.quote: Is that something y'all do? It's something I think I could probably do myself, actually.
cherry_pick_moments.stewart_read: Larry's ground-mount question is his peak curiosity moment — he has observed a neighbor's installation, identified a technical workaround for his east-west roof, and is actively problem-solving. This is an enthusiasm signal and a scope-creep trap simultaneously. The enthusiasm: Larry is genuinely interested in solar if the technical fit exists. The trap: Jake answered the question directly ('we actually don't do the ground mount systems anymore') instead of bridging it to the specialist who can actually address it. This triggered the roof-leak warranty debate that followed — a 90-second exchange Jake did not win.
cherry_pick_moments.alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake may have wanted to close off ground mounts quickly to keep the conversation on roof-mount systems where Ion can deliver. Partially plausible, but the execution (flat denial + unsolicited warranty defense) closed off Larry's curiosity rather than redirecting it.
cherry_pick_moments.coaching_implication: Use this moment to teach the specialist bridge. Suggested reframe (intent, not verbatim): 'Ground mounts are worth exploring — that's a design question our specialist can work through with your actual site data. Want me to get you on with them?' The bridge converts the technical question into a booking reason rather than a debate topic.

cherry_pick_moments.quote: Yeah. I mean, you got our number, so you can give us a callback if, it is something that you're kinda trying to look into when, you do get that roof. Alright?
cherry_pick_moments.stewart_read: This is the highest-revenue moment Jake missed. Larry has just said he cannot consider solar until he gets a new roof — and that the roof is scheduled but delayed by snow. The codex covers this exactly: a customer who needs a new roof is a roofing lead AND a deferred solar lead, not a lost contact. Ion does roofing in-house. Jake's response — 'you got our number, give us a callback' — is a passive surrender that produces zero revenue and leaves Larry without any reason to call back. The roofing bridge (per cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned) would have converted a 'not ready for solar' into an immediate roofing conversation and a locked solar follow-up.
cherry_pick_moments.alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake assessed Larry as too skeptical to convert on this call and decided a soft exit was better than a hard push. Not supported — there is no evidence Jake knew about Ion's roofing offering. The more likely read is that Jake simply did not know the bridge existed.
cherry_pick_moments.coaching_implication: This is the primary teaching moment for the manager. Open the codex to cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned and show Jake the exact bridge language. Then ask: 'What would a successful outcome look like here if we can't book a solar appointment today?' The answer is a roofing referral and a locked solar callback — not a 'give us a call.' This miss cost Ion a roofing sale and a solar pipeline entry.

cherry_pick_moments.quote: Yeah. It is it is a
cherry_pick_moments.stewart_read: Larry has just said data mining is 'the biggest money making thing in the world today' in a tone of resigned accusation. Jake's response — 'Yeah. It is' — is a full concession that aligns Jake with the adversarial frame Larry is using. This is the lowest trust point of the call. The codex protocol for this concern calls for honest specificity that reduces suspicion — not agreement with the customer's most hostile interpretation. Jake had no reframe prepared and defaulted to social agreement, which confirmed Larry's view of Ion as a data-mining operation.
cherry_pick_moments.alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake was trying to validate Larry's frustration to build rapport. Not supported — validating 'data mining is the biggest money-making thing in the world' does not build rapport with a customer who has named Ion as a participant in that system.
cherry_pick_moments.coaching_implication: Show Jake the contrast between protocols.how_did_you_get_my_number (honest specificity reduces suspicion) and what he actually said. Then ask: 'When a customer says something like this, what's the one thing we never want to do?' Agreement is the wrong move. A better orientation: acknowledge the frustration, separate Ion from the ad that misled him, and bring the conversation back to what Larry actually wanted — which was solar.

cherry_pick_moments.quote: Alright. Well, maybe I'll contact you after I, get a roof. Cool. You don't even have an idea of what what these are running?
cherry_pick_moments.stewart_read: Larry's 'maybe I'll contact you after I get a roof' is a soft callback — not a locked time, not a locked commitment. The codex's soft_outcome_distinction notes the major delta between a callback with a locked time and 'we'll touch base.' Jake accepts it without any attempt to convert it to a real follow-up structure. The follow-on pricing question is then answered with a partial explanation of the lease model — more scope creep — rather than being bridged to the specialist as the reason to book. This moment was the last window to convert a passive exit into an actionable next step.
cherry_pick_moments.alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake recognized the call was over and was managing a graceful exit. Plausible, but the strategic version of that exit would have locked the roofing referral and a specific callback date before hanging up.
cherry_pick_moments.coaching_implication: Ask Jake: 'What's the difference between a customer saying they'll call us back and us having a real next step?' Use this to introduce the locked-callback structure — even if the appointment is weeks out (pending the roof), the rep should set a specific date and send a confirmation text. Without a locked time, this lead is cold the moment the call ends.

coaching_focus.quote: Yeah. I mean, you got our number, so you can give us a callback if, it is something that you're kinda trying to look into when, you do get that roof. Alright?
coaching_focus.topic: Cross-sell bridge discipline — roof objection as roofing lead
coaching_focus.why: The roof-before-solar objection is one of the most common disqualifiers in this industry, and Ion's in-house roofing capability converts it from a dead end into an immediate revenue event plus a locked solar pipeline entry. Jake did not deploy this bridge — likely because he did not know it existed. Until Jake internalizes this move, every roof-objection call ends the same way: passive exit, zero revenue. The roofing bridge also preserves the deferred solar relationship; without it, Larry has no structural reason to call Ion back when his roof is done.

coaching_focus.quote: So the only thing I remember signing up for or or filling out was for Tesla. Are you are you, like, a subcontractor of Tesla?
coaching_focus.topic: Tesla-sourced lead identity reframe — deploy the codex script
coaching_focus.why: Larry raised the Tesla-identity question four times. Jake never used the clean reframe the codex provides for Tesla-sourced leads. The result was that Larry spent the entire call operating on a false frame (Tesla was giving away free solar roofs) that Jake could not replace because he had no structured answer for it. This is a mechanics gap — Jake needs the two-sentence Tesla-partner reframe committed to memory so it deploys on the first ask, not the fourth.
